text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CLINIC — OFFICE VISIT NOTE
Patient: Renata Okonkwo
Diagnosis: Stage IV (M1c) Malignant Melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for reassessment following disease progression on dabrafenib/trametinib. New right-sided neck mass and worsening right upper quadrant discomfort. Results of repeat biopsy and liquid biopsy reviewed.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 55-year-old woman with a well-established history of Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma (primary site: mid-back, T4bN3cM1c, Breslow 5.8 mm, ulcerated, mitotic rate 8/mm², Clark level V), initially presenting approximately 8 months ago with widespread cutaneous/subcutaneous nodules, bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy, and multiple hepatic metastases. She was initiated on dabrafenib 150 mg BID plus trametinib 2 mg daily in the first-line setting given her high tumor burden, BRAF V600E positivity, and desire for rapid disease control.

Her initial course on dab/tram was notable for a Grade 2 fever episode at approximately 6 weeks (dabrafenib briefly held, fully resolved) and development of two cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas on the left forearm and right shin at approximately 7 months (managed by dermatology with simple excision, margins clear). She achieved a marked early response at the 8-week restaging scan — right axillary node down from 4.2 cm to 1.1 cm, hepatic lesions substantially reduced — and continued to deepen her response through the 6-month restaging.

Unfortunately, at approximately 8 months, she began reporting new right-sided neck fullness and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort. She was brought in for urgent imaging, which demonstrated unequivocal disease progression: a new 2.2 cm right cervical Level II lymph node with central necrosis, re-enlargement of the right axillary node to 2.8 cm, three new hepatic lesions in the right lobe (0.8–1.5 cm), growth of the residual segment 6 hepatic lesion (now 1.4 cm from 0.6 cm), and a new peritoneal nodule near the falciform ligament (0.9 cm). MRI brain was negative for intracranial metastases. LDH rose from the nadir of 156 to 389 U/L.

A core needle biopsy of the right cervical lymph node was performed and confirmed metastatic melanoma (SOX10+, S100+, HMB-45+, Ki-67 ~55%). Concurrently, a liquid biopsy (Guardant360 ctDNA) was sent, which returned showing persistence of the BRAF V600E mutation (VAF 3.2%) along with two acquired resistance alterations: a MAP2K1 (MEK1) p.C121S mutation (VAF 1.8%) and BRAF amplification. These molecular findings are entirely consistent with classic MAPK pathway reactivation driving acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition. Notably, on her original Foundation One CDx panel, she harbored concurrent PTEN loss and an NF1 splice site mutation — a co-mutation pattern we discussed at the time of treatment initiation as carrying higher risk for earlier resistance, which has borne out clinically.

Dabrafenib and trametinib have been discontinued as of today.

Today's visit focuses on confirmation of the next therapeutic step: transition to ipilimumab plus nivolumab combination immunotherapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Fatigue, moderate, worsening over the past 2–3 weeks. Decreased appetite. No fever or chills currently.
- HEENT: Right-sided neck fullness and mild discomfort, noted since approximately 3 weeks ago. No dysphagia.
- Gastrointestinal: Intermittent right upper quadrant aching, non-colicky, no jaundice, no nausea/vomiting. Bowel habits baseline 1–2 formed stools per day — patient specifically asked and confirms this. No hematochezia.
- Dermatologic: No new suspicious skin lesions. The two excision sites from the cutaneous SCCs are well-healed. No rash, no pruritic lesions.
- Musculoskeletal: Generalized myalgia, mild, attributed to fatigue and disease activity.
- Endocrine: Continued mild fatigue — some overlap with hypothyroidism; TSH was within normal range 8 weeks ago on levothyroxine 75 mcg.
- Neurologic: No headaches, no visual changes, no focal deficits.
- Respiratory: No cough, no dyspnea.
- Genitourinary: Normal.
- Psychiatric: Anxious about disease progression but coping with support from family. Engaged and asking detailed questions.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Alert, oriented, well-nourished. Mildly fatigued appearing. ECOG performance status 1.
Vitals: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 16, Temp 36.9°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 68 kg (down ~2 kg from last visit).
Head/Neck: Right Level II cervical lymph node palpable, firm, non-tender, approximately 2 cm. No left cervical adenopathy. No supraclavicular nodes. Oropharynx clear. Thyroid non-enlarged.
Axillae: Right axillary adenopathy palpable, firm cluster, estimated 2.5–3 cm clinically, mildly tender on deep palpation. Left axilla without palpable adenopathy.
Skin: Well-healed excision scar on left forearm and right shin. No residual palpable subcutaneous nodules on trunk or extremities. No new pigmented lesions of concern on brief inspection (formal dermatology exam performed 6 weeks prior — clear at that time).
Abdomen: Soft. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant. No rebound or guarding. Liver percussed to approximately 13 cm span; edge mildly palpable on deep inspiration. No splenomegaly. No ascites detected.
Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion. No joint swelling or synovitis.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Gait normal.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 6.8 K/µL (diff: 68% neutrophils, 22% lymphocytes, 8% monocytes), Hgb 11.9 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia), platelets 198 K/µL.
CMP: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.85 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 94 mg/dL.
LFTs: AST 68 U/L (elevated, ~1.5x ULN), ALT 72 U/L (elevated, ~1.5x ULN), total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL (normal), alkaline phosphatase 167 U/L (elevated). Albumin 3.8 g/dL.
LDH: 389 U/L (elevated, ~1.9x ULN) — rising from nadir of 156 U/L; consistent with significant disease progression.
TSH: 1.9 mIU/L (within normal limits on levothyroxine 75 mcg).
Coagulation: INR 1.1, within normal limits.
HBsAg: Negative. Anti-HBc: Negative. Anti-HCV: Negative (reviewed prior; not repeated today).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain with gadolinium (obtained approximately 1 week ago): As detailed in the prior note, findings are consistent with unambiguous progression on BRAF/MEK inhibition. New right Level II cervical node (2.2 cm, necrotic), right axillary node re-enlarged to 2.8 cm, three new hepatic right lobe metastases (0.8–1.5 cm), worsening of prior hepatic lesion (segment 6, now 1.4 cm), new peritoneal nodule at the falciform ligament (0.9 cm). MRI brain negative for intracranial disease.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IV BRAF V600E-MUTANT METASTATIC MELANOMA — PROGRESSION ON FIRST-LINE DABRAFENIB/TRAMETINIB

Ms. Okonkwo has experienced clinical and radiographic progression after an approximately 8-month response to dabrafenib/trametinib, which is broadly consistent with the median PFS historically observed with this regimen. The acquired resistance profile on liquid biopsy — MEK1 C121S mutation and BRAF amplification — confirms MAPK pathway reactivation as the resistance mechanism. This is not retrievable with re-challenge of the same BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination.

We have had an extended discussion today about second-line treatment options. The strongest option for this patient is combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab (ipi) plus nivolumab (nivo) based on the CheckMate-067 data demonstrating a 5-year overall survival of approximately 52% with this combination in advanced melanoma. Key factors favoring ipi/nivo in her case:
   - High TMB: 28 mut/Mb (high, favors immunotherapy benefit)
   - PD-L1 TPS 15% (borderline, does not preclude benefit)
   - MSS status (no MSI-H to specifically enhance benefit beyond TMB)
   - No prior immunotherapy exposure
   - ECOG PS 1 — acceptable for combination immunotherapy
   - No active autoimmune disease; prior uveitis remote and quiescent
   - LFTs mildly elevated but likely driven by hepatic metastases rather than autoimmune hepatitis; baseline documented

Risks of ipi/nivo discussed extensively today, including immune-related adverse events (irAEs): colitis (occurring in ~30% Grade 3+ with ipi/nivo combination), hepatitis, pneumonitis, hypophysitis, thyroiditis, nephritis, rare but severe events including myocarditis. Patient understands the profile and the potential need for steroids and/or immunosuppression. She has been given detailed written instructions on when to call the clinic or go to the ED, particularly regarding diarrhea. Importantly, we have noted her baseline stool frequency of 1–2 formed stools per day so we have a clear comparator if diarrhea develops. She will be given a steroid prescription and action plan card.

Plan:
   - Initiate ipilimumab 3 mg/kg + nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg flat dose every 4 weeks as maintenance (per CheckMate-067 design)
   - Pre-medicate with acetaminophen and diphenhydramine per institutional protocol
   - Baseline LFTs, CBC, CMP, TSH today (reviewed above)
   - Repeat LFTs at each infusion visit; check TSH every 6 weeks
   - Ophthalmology referral made to establish baseline given history of prior anterior uveitis; patient to alert clinic to any visual changes immediately
   - No hepatitis B or C reactivation risk (serologies negative)
   - Dabrafenib/trametinib discontinued as of today; patient has stopped taking both agents as of this morning

2. MILDLY ELEVATED LIVER FUNCTION TESTS
AST 68, ALT 72, ALP 167 — attributed to hepatic metastatic disease. Total bilirubin normal and albumin preserved, no evidence of synthetic dysfunction. Will monitor closely at each infusion visit. If LFTs worsen after initiating ipi/nivo, will need to distinguish disease progression from immune-related hepatitis (threshold: Grade 2 or higher AST/ALT elevation would prompt withholding). Hepatology aware.

3. MILD NORMOCYTIC ANEMIA
Hgb 11.9 g/dL, likely related to chronic disease/cancer-related anemia. No evidence of hemolysis or active bleeding. Iron studies will be checked at next visit. Will monitor; transfusion not indicated at this time.

4. HYPOTHYROIDISM
Stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg. TSH 1.9 mIU/L — currently well-controlled. Will continue current dose. Given risk of immune-related thyroid dysfunction with nivo-containing regimens (and patient's underlying hypothyroidism creating added vulnerability), TSH will be monitored every 6 weeks. Endocrinology kept in the loop.

5. WEIGHT LOSS (2 KG)
Modest, likely multifactorial — decreased appetite in the setting of disease progression and emotional stress. Nutritional counseling offered. No parenteral nutrition indicated. Will monitor at each visit.

6. DERMATOLOGIC SURVEILLANCE
Next dermatology appointment in 8 weeks. The risk of cutaneous SCCs is specific to the BRAF inhibitor class; with discontinuation of dabrafenib, this risk diminishes. However, nivo can rarely cause lichenoid reactions or vitiligo (the latter, when it occurs, has been associated with better outcomes in melanoma). Patient aware.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL
Patient is understandably anxious about disease progression after a period of excellent response. She was encouraged to continue engaging with her support network. Social work referral offered (patient deferred for now but will consider). She is motivated and has a clear understanding of the treatment rationale.

Return to clinic in 3 weeks for cycle 1 of ipi/nivo. Patient to call at any time for new symptoms — emphasis placed on diarrhea, rash, dyspnea, visual changes, headache, severe fatigue. Emergency contact information reinforced.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 55 minutes.

[Dictated and signed by Attending Medical Oncologist]
